sentences.text: That's right, and when I came back, my purse was gone.
I had no identification, no money.
All the important documents were in my purse.
So I let word out, and the-- and the Scotland Yard were very, very kind.
They let word out that I do not mind for them to keep the money and my purse.

I had a crocodile purse, very expensive purse, and I told them they can even keep my purse, but the-- my identification will not help them in any way, form, or shape, but they are very important to me.
Two days later, I found them wrapped in newspaper, and I was able then to call the Scotland Yard and they then examined my papers and declared me as a friendly alien.
Now, we were all separated in different groups because no one organization or no one hostel could care-- could take care of all of us.
There must have been about 120.
So--
Only women.
And so four ladies that we got to know in Sandwich held onto me for life and said wherever Irmy goes, that's where we want to go.
So we went, and we were assigned to the worst hostel ever you can imagine.
It was in the worst part of London, [INAUDIBLE] Street.
It was more or less a whore house, and the-- the-- the-- these women had cubicles there, but we never got to see them.
But we four women got one room.
That woman who owned it and operated it was a little bit crazy.
She didn't give us any food.
I lost so much weight.
I got 85 pounds.
She was crazy.
It was a hostel they called it, like a boarding house.
Some time.
So what happened, in the meantime, the Germans started to bomb, and where we lived was the first place they bombed.
Now, this woman got a yellow light whenever the planes crossed the Channel.
And at that time, she would come and wake us up and grab us by the hair, and said, we have to go underground.
So we would all go underground.
It's light came from a place on the coast to warn England that bombs are approaching and coming to bomb London.
And she was in that group who received the warning.
No, it's very hard.
It was at the beginning.
Right at the beginning.
No, it must have been 1940.
So what happened, so we all ran out in a hurry.
And there was about half a block a church, and underneath the church was a big cellar and we would all congregate there.
Now, this cellar actually only accommodated 200 people, but we were 600, and sometimes the workers would bring in half bodies of people who were bombed, and they wouldn't leave them on the street.
It was the most dreadful, horrible experience.
In the meantime, when I was assigned to that hostel, it was not suitable for my baby to be there.
So they took my baby in a children's home because where she can get proper attention.
But that children's home was near an RAF office and they were afraid that the Germans will bomb the RAF office and took the children's home to the country to [?
Bedford, ?] and I was not allowed to go and see her.
So here I was--
I beg your-- No, she was about-- yeah, yeah.
So she was taken there, and I was now, all by myself with these four women.
So what we did-- I did not open my mouth because I didn't want to speak.
They would recognize I'm German.
I speak with a German accent, and I was afraid here of being bombed by Germans.
What will happen to me?
I didn't.
I got myself a knitting material and was knitting for my baby.
And I always managed to-- to be close to the door.
And we were sitting there like-- like sandwiched in.
And then, sometimes we had to stay there all night long without any food until the air raid was over, and we went back to the hostel.
And that happened about three, four times, and then finally, they bombed our area to the ground.
The only place was left that church.
Everything else was gone.
Our hostel was half gone.
I was able to get a little part of my belongings and so my girl.
And finally, they transported us to Golders Green, which was a beautiful, beautiful part of London.
And this hostel, or this home belonged to Jewish people.
It was a hostel.
In Golders Green.
Yeah, Golders Green.
It was one of the nicest places.
It was like, thank God.
That's what it was because they cooked and they did everything.
So now, we have to do some work.
And what I had done in Germany-- I knew that I could not be a legal secretary anywhere because my English was not good enough and my stenography was not-- I wrote stenography.
It was not good enough.
So my hobby was making hats.
So what I had done after I worked and for the Czechoslovakian firm, I had a woman milliner come to my house at night and teach me how to make hats.
This was back in Berlin.
In Berlin.
When then, they-- they came to us and we were interviewed again by the-- by the Scotland Yard to ascertain whether we were alien-- friendly aliens or enemy aliens.
They declared us to be friendly aliens.
So they allowed us to work.
So what they did, they packed up a truck, took us all women on a truck to nearby village.
And there was a company like Campbell's Soup.
They made all kinds of canned food, and I-- and we were supposed to clean, peel the potatoes, peel the carrots, peel everything.
And they would pay us and would give us room and board.
And I was the only one who said, no, I am not going to work here.
You can make money.
How come that you don't want to work here?
I said no, that's not my profession.
So the three girls again held onto me, and they said, if she doesn't work here, I'm not going to work here.
So they took us back to London.
I applied for a millinery job, and I got an interview with a wholesale millinery company, Jewish people owning it, Mr.
And Mrs.
[INAUDIBLE].
So when she said, can you copy a hat?
I said, I will try.
And she gave me a hood and asked me to copy that hat.
And after finished, she threw it in the garbage.
It was no good.
Then she gave me a hood and she said, you make whatever you want with that hood, and I--
Hood is an unfinished hat, the raw material to make a hat.
Yeah, and it was like a hood, you know, with the-- the brim and the top.
And so she gave me that hat and I made what I wanted.
After I was finished, she threw it in the garbage.
So I knew that-- my lunchtime was on.
I knew that was good bye, Charlie.
When I came back from lunch, all the girls were laughing.
And I said, what is so funny?
Because I'm going to get my-- my papers to leave?
They said, no, you won't believe it.
What happened, a buyer came in and wanted to buy hats from her.
And she had a designer who designed hats, and she showed him all the hats.
He did not buy one.
Then she takes my hat from the garbage, and he ordered two dozen.
And that saved my life.
So she realized that I had idea.
I was artistic, but I didn't know the mechanics.
So she taught me for one week the mechanics.
And I tell you, I almost thought I fainted because the steam from the hats with all the chemicals just were atrocious, but I did it.
And I became a milliner copyist.
So after about eight weeks, I asked for more money because I couldn't make a go.
I couldn't get enough money to supply-- to support myself.
She said, how can I give you more money when the others work here already for 10 years for the same money?
I said, well, they live at home, but I don't have a home.
So she said, I tell you what.
I'm going to make a suggestion.
Everybody who makes more than four hats a day gets $0.50 per hat.
That's a good deal.
Then we don't hurt anyone.
I made eight hats, and the others made four hats.
And so I got more money.
So-- Now, how were you communicating with Sonia at this point?
She was--
I-- I-- I called them, and they said Sonia was all right, but she wasn't.
She was very lonesome for us.
So then--
I didn't know.
I didn't know.
So then, I come back to this-- then, when-- when I became a copyist, I wanted to copy the designer's hats, and she didn't want me to copy her hat.
So I took one, a hood, which I was allowed to do, and I made a hat for myself, exactly copied the designer's hat.
So my boss said, Irmy, you-- you do a beautiful job on Theresa's hats.
You can copy them.
So I copied them.
Anyhow, in the meantime, the designer got married and she gave up her job, and I became the designer of that company.
Now, with Hugo-- there was another couple in that hostel, an elderly couple that did not speak English at all.
And they had to go to their lawyer to help the lawyer get them the papers to go to America.
They had all the papers, but they could not communicate in English.
So they asked me if I would go with them.
In this meantime, I spoke better English because the girls in the-- in my job helped me a lot and corrected me and helped me.
And I started speaking better and better.
So I went.
And then the gentleman said to me, how much do-- when the conference was over-- how much do I owe you?
I said, you don't owe me anything.
He says, I beg your pardon.
You did the translating.
I couldn't have done it without you.
I said look, there is a time to take money and there is a time to work for love.
This is a labor of love.
He said, what happened to your husband?
Where is your husband?
I said, he was taken somewhere, but I don't know.
He says, I'm going to tell you something.
I know where he was taken.
I'm going to get him out for you without any charge.
And he came out from the Isle of Wight.
That's where he was.
And what was he doing on the Isle of Wight?
Huh?
They were protecting them.
They didn't want the Nazis to know where these men were, that they should put again some spies there.
That's why it was kept secret.
Nobody was supposed to know.
Yes, all the men.
And so Hugo was released, and he was informed where I was.
And by the way, before then, I got a call to come, to tell me-- my four girls that we-- the men had signed up for Australia.
And I-- and they want to send us to Australia.
I-- I couldn't correspond with my husband.
I couldn't communicate with him to find out whether it was true or not, but my sense said, out of question because my husband's family live in America.
America was our dream.
Why would I want to go to Australia?
I said, no, I'm not going.
So the four women, again, held onto me and they didn't go.
That boat got into a mine or something and blew up and all of them were killed.
That's the truth.
And we were safe.
What was the name of that boat?
Do you know?
I don't know.
I remember Lisa Salonda.
She's dead now.
And Marcus and Kennedy and myself, we were four.
Marcus was their last name.
I forgot their first name.
Lisa Salonda she was-- her husband was also in the Kitchener camp and later on, released.
He was the son of the concert master of the Philharmonic in Vienna.
And we then-- and then, when I got the job and Hugo got out, and Lisa Salonda was then-- she got a job in a company who made buttons.
And then when-- when Eric Salonda came out, we-- she found out that there was a beautiful, beautiful apartment, that-- furnished apartment that she and I-- we and-- and them can share.
And she found it and we found-- and we then moved into the apartment.
When we moved into the apartment--
In London.
We picked up Sonia, and she came then to live with us.
It was very beautiful.
No, when you met your daughter, when you went to see your daughter and took her away.
She was the happiest child on this earth.
She had wanted to come so badly that it wasn't even funny.
She must have been three, three and a half, because she had some kind of a sickness, and they took her away to the hospital.
And she had no family.
It was some kind of pneumonia, I think.
She was sick, yes.
But she-- she made it, and we got her back.
And so we were then a family, thank God.
And she then lived with us.
I think she was, but the fact that she didn't have-- she knew already about her parents and didn't have her parents there was very distressing for her.
She didn't like that at all.
She never was really happy in that-- in that children's home.
Terrible, but we had no choice.
We didn't make any money, and we had no choice.
So we had to.
But when my husband came out, he got-- he was offered a job to clear the debris from the bombing.
And my husband said, what do you think about it?
I said, I wouldn't do it.
He said, that's good money.
I said, money here, money there.
Your hands are your future.
If, God forbid, something happens to your hand, that is the end of that story.
So he found a job as a watchmaker in a jewelry store, and he worked as a watchmaker.
And in the afternoons, we had our own clientele.
We then made sure because watchmakers were very, very hard to come by.
We had the most important customers you ever can imagine.
There was one customer, he was from the Scotland Yard.
I never forget that.
When my husband worked already, and he brought us some watches, and we repaired them.
And one day, he wanted us to go to his home in the country by train.
And he said, and I want you to take a little trunk along and keep it locked.
Give it to my mother and never open it before you get home.
And my husband went there and gave them the trunk and repaired the clock and came back, no, no problems.
And when we opened, it was full of chickens, which we hadn't seen in years, eggs-- we hadn't seen in years-- butter, and everything.
So we shared it with our friends because otherwise, it gets bad.
Well, I tell you, we were received-- once they found out who we were, they were nice.
In fact, to be very honest with you, the neighbors of the apartment we lived in were very strange.
They came from the Isle of Wight, and they did not believe in foreigners.
They were very, very funny.
And when one day, I-- she knocked at the door-- the back door-- and she said someone left a package.
She gave us a package.
I'll make it fast.
And then a couple of months later, she knocked again.
She said, what in heaven are you cooking?
It smells so good.
Because the English people cook very flat, and I told her what it is.
Then, the next time she called up, she came again on the back door, my husband wants to be officially introduced to you.
And I had to make a-- a afternoon tea with crumpets and all the-- the things, the good things that the English people have, in the afternoon.
And he came.
I never had met him before.
And they came in, and we became the closest friends.
He called me his daughter.
He gave me and Hugo a key to their apartment, and we came.
We walked in without any problems.
He wrote a book on Egyptian history, and he gave me to read the book.
I said, what-- why do you want me to take-- read that book?
He said, I want your honest opinion.
When-- he gave me the manuscript.
And when I returned it, he said, what do you think?
I said, well, now, do you want my honest opinion or do you want me to say it's beautiful?
No, I want your honest opinion.
So I pointed out all the things that sounded unusual to me.
I said don't forget I'm a layman.
I'm not in-- a history major.
So you want to write the book for everyone to read.
So it has to be so that the average person will understand what you are talking about.
He changed every bit that I made a note of, and we became the closest friends.
I still have some jewelry that they gave me when they moved to their own home in later years.
And when he passed away, my husband and I were asked to be in the family room on the day of the funeral.
So you're telling a story that indicates that you and your husband and Sonia made a little life for yourself that was OK in London.
Did the bombs keep dropping?
Yes, the bombs had stopped because they were invading Poland.
And so we got a little relief.
After that, the bombing started in again.
And while I went always into shelters, but you know, after a while I got tired of it.
So my-- so my daughter would, in her dream, stand up for me to take it and run.
And we ran then in a shelter next to our apartment.
It was about a six-apartment building.
And we would do that.
And then, that night, part of London was bombed only a block and a half away from us where friends of ours were living.
The whole building was smithereen, and that's when my husband said, enough is enough.
He sent me and Sonia to the movies, and he packed everything up and sent it to North Wales, where were no bombing.
We had spent a vacation in North Wales with Hugo prior.
North Wales.
Llanfyllin, that was near Oswestry.
Llanfyllin.
And he packed everything up, called the hotel, made reservation, and we stayed-- and when we came home, I said, what happened?
He said, we are leaving.
I'm not staying here in London.
I want to live.
And we packed up and the next time, by train, we went to North Wales.
My husband worked in the hotel in our room.
They were very kind.
They gave him a bench.
And he set up a watchmaker.
And he made a watch.
And his boss sent him all the watches to Wales.
Two years.
It was so bad that we had all our friends to come to Llanfyllin.
His boss came.
We got them accommodation in hotels and farmers and friends.
Very well, excellent.
Again, we were asked by the police department to-- because we should have been on a curfew, you know, because we were Germans.
And the police department was informed.
In England, everything is recorded that we are coming to Wales.
So the police department came to the Eagle Hotel and interviewed us.
And he said, you don't have to go by curfew.
You can-- you-- you are accepted here, open-hearted.
And so we-- Hugo worked.
There were no Jews-- and then, after a while, my friends had to go back.
They got a little relief from the bombing.
It was heavy bombing.
And they got a little relief, and they went back.
And we stayed on in Wales until after the war, until 1945.
And it was very funny.
We-- there were no Jews, only one couple.
And they were-- they-- they-- both of them worked for a big farmer.
She was in the kitchen, and he did farm work.
But we had very little contact with anyone.
But I found out my holidays and when my holidays came, like Rosh Hoshana, Yom Kippur, we didn't work.
We went into the hills.
They had mountains, and we celebrated by ourselves in the hills.
And we-- woods in Yom Kippur.
We wouldn't eat.
We all had a beautiful dinner when we came home.
No [INAUDIBLE], no meat, nothing.
And it was very nice, and the people were very, very nice.
And one more thing I will say.
They had a daughter, who had a cafe, and she catered--
Yes, and she-- Sonia went to school in Wales.
Sonia spoke perfect Welsh.
She must have been six, and she started to speak a beautiful Welsh.
She could speak Welsh.
And then the daughter of-- of the people who owned the hotel had a cafe, and she catered to the-- she catered to the Army, the American Army who were there.
And every Sunday, she would give a-- a party for them-- for the American soldiers.
They were not far from Oswestry.
They would come to Llanfyllin, and she would have sandwiches and fruit.
And we would volunteer to help to make the sandwiches and that was strictly volunteer.
Then, I volunteered to be a Red Cross nurse.
And once a week, I went to Oswestry by train and worked in a hospital as a volunteer.
And-- and then I came home.
And there were lots of people I could help.
For instance, when they came in the recovery room, that was all on a voluntary basis.
They wanted me-- the doctors, particularly, wanted me to be there when they were operating, but my train didn't come in early enough so that I could only be there after the operation.
